service-as-software.headline: Play service-as-software: sell the outcome, not the chatbot
service-as-software.whoItsFor: Builders who can ship automation and sell to SMBs. You need sales more than another model wrapper.
service-as-software.firstDollar: Days to weeks if you can close a pilot. Price a monthly outcome retainer, not hourly tinkering.

steps.title: 1. Pick one vertical pain
steps.body: Home services, clinics, agencies, ecom ops. One job: booked appointments, recovered failed payments, invoice chase, lead follow-up. Horizontal 'AI for everything' is dead meta.

steps.title: 2. Productize the outcome
steps.body: Write a one-page offer: input data, weekly deliverable, KPI, price. Example: 'We book 20 qualified calls / mo or you do not pay month two.'

steps.title: 3. Run 5 manual pilots
steps.body: Do it scrappy with Zapier/Make/n8n + human QA. Proof of ROI beats a pretty agent demo.

steps.title: 4. Turn the runbook into a retainer
steps.body: Standardize onboarding, monitoring, and a monthly report. Move off hourly. Keep humans on the edge cases.

steps.title: 5. Ladder toward software
steps.body: If the same workflow repeats, wrap it. That is the path from agency to micro-SaaS without building in the dark.

pitfalls: Selling 'we do AI' instead of a metric the buyer already cares about
No access to the client's systems = no outcome

productized-service.headline: Play productized service: one menu, fixed price
productized-service.whoItsFor: Freelancers who already get clients but bleed time on custom quotes and scope creep.
productized-service.firstDollar: Weeks if you already have a skill buyers pay for. First sale is a repackaged version of work you have done.

steps.title: 1. Name the single offer
steps.body: One deliverable, one price, one turnaround. Kill the 'custom quote' reflex. DesignJoy energy: a menu, not a consulting firm.

steps.title: 2. Write the scope wall
steps.body: What is in, what is out, revision limits, SLA. Put it on a simple landing page with a buy or apply button.

steps.title: 3. Sell before you overbuild
steps.body: DM past clients and warm network. Close 3 at the new price. Delivery can stay semi-manual at first.

steps.title: 4. Template the delivery
steps.body: Checklists, Figma kits, code scaffolds, report shells. Your hours per job must fall every month.

steps.title: 5. Raise price or add a second SKU
steps.body: Only after utilization is full. Do not expand the menu early.

pitfalls: Fake productization that is still fully custom
Underpricing because you fear saying no
No waitlist, so you never raise rates

indie-micro-saas.headline: Play indie / micro-SaaS: boring niche, recurring revenue
indie-micro-saas.whoItsFor: Builders who can ship and market. Coding is the easy half. Distribution is the boss fight.
indie-micro-saas.firstDollar: Often 3-18 months. Many never clear ramen. Plan for unpaid build + marketing time.

steps.title: 1. Hunt a paid pain
steps.body: Talk to people who already spend money. Prefer boring B2B niches over consumer toys. Scrapers, compliance, reporting, recovery tools beat 'AI journal'.

steps.title: 2. Pre-sell or waitlist
steps.body: Landing page + Stripe or email waitlist before a full build. If nobody clicks, you saved months.

steps.title: 3. Ship the ugly v1
steps.body: One core job. Auth, billing, the workflow. Launch in public. Instrument usage.

steps.title: 4. Own a distribution channel
steps.body: SEO, directories, communities, partnerships, or content. Ads optional. No channel = no SaaS.

steps.title: 5. Kill or double down
steps.body: If you cannot reach ~1k MRR with honest effort, shelf it. Portfolio of small bets beats one sunk-cost zombie.

pitfalls: Building for other indie hackers only
Feature bloat before first 10 paying users
Treating Twitter screenshots as median income

freelancing.headline: Play freelancing: skill in, invoice out
freelancing.whoItsFor: Anyone with a marketable skill who needs cash now. Best starter ladder, not the final form.
freelancing.firstDollar: Days to weeks with outbound and a clear offer. Platforms are slow without reviews.

steps.title: 1. Pick a sellable skill
steps.body: Writing, design, dev, video, bookkeeping, ads. Narrow the niche (e.g. 'landing pages for dentists') so buyers feel understood.

steps.title: 2. Proof pack
steps.body: 3 portfolio pieces even if speculative. One case study with a before/after metric.

steps.title: 3. Outbound daily
steps.body: 20-40 targeted messages per day to people who already spend. Platforms are optional. Direct is faster.

steps.title: 4. Deliver and ask for the next job
steps.body: Under-promise, hit dates, request referrals. Raise rates every few wins.

steps.title: 5. Productize or stay stuck
steps.body: After consistent clients, move to productized-service or an agency. Pure freelance caps on your hours.

pitfalls: No niche, so every pitch is generic

email-list-funnel.headline: Play the email list: own the relationship
email-list-funnel.whoItsFor: Creators and operators who refuse to rent all their audience from an algorithm.
email-list-funnel.firstDollar: Weeks to months after you have a lead magnet and an offer. A list with no offer is a hobby.

steps.title: 1. One audience, one promise
steps.body: Who and what outcome. 'Weekly money meta for operators' beats 'thoughts on life'.

steps.title: 2. Lead magnet that is worth an email
steps.body: Checklist, template, mini-tool, or sample issue. Host on your domain. ESP: Beehiiv, ConvertKit, or similar.

steps.title: 3. Publish on a cadence
steps.body: Weekly is enough. Teach something concrete. Soft CTAs every issue.

steps.title: 4. Attach an offer
steps.body: Digital product, service, affiliate, or community. Without an offer the list is vanity.

steps.title: 5. Grow via content, not only paid
steps.body: Short-form, SEO, collaborations. Paid ads only after unit economics work.

pitfalls: Collecting emails with nothing to sell

short-form-content-funnel.headline: Play short-form as a funnel, not a career
short-form-content-funnel.whoItsFor: People who can post consistently and already know what they will sell off-platform.
short-form-content-funnel.firstDollar: Platform funds alone are a trap. First real dollar comes from the offer behind the clip.

steps.title: 1. Decide the destination
steps.body: Email, YouTube, booking link, or product. Every video needs a next step. Views without a pipe are a hobby.

steps.title: 2. Pick a repeatable format
steps.body: Hooks + one idea + CTA. Batch film. Post daily for 30-90 days before judging.

steps.title: 3. Route traffic off-app
steps.body: Link in bio to one page. Do not scatter CTAs. Measure email signups or sales, not likes.

steps.title: 4. Double down on winners
steps.body: Remake top hooks. Kill formats that do not convert to the destination.

steps.title: 5. Never treat the fund as salary
steps.body: Creativity Program and similar payouts are tip money. Build owned monetization.

pitfalls: Burnout from infinite posting without systems
Believing CPM is a business model

youtube-channel.headline: Play YouTube as a business, not AdSense cosplay
youtube-channel.whoItsFor: Long-form creators who can ship weekly and build an ecosystem around the channel.
youtube-channel.firstDollar: Often 12-36 months to meaningful revenue. Partner Program is the floor, not the ceiling.

steps.title: 1. Niche + promise
steps.body: A viewer should know what they get every upload. Searchable topics help early; personality compounds later.

steps.title: 2. Upload cadence and packaging
steps.body: Thumbnails and titles are half the game. Study CTR. Consistency beats perfect production.

steps.title: 3. Monetize in layers
steps.body: Ads, then sponsors, then your product/list. MrBeast-scale is not the plan; a small niche business is.

steps.title: 4. Capture the audience
steps.body: Every video should grow something you own: email, Discord, or product waitlist.

steps.title: 5. Kill dead series fast
steps.body: Analytics over ego. Double the formats that retain and convert.

newsletter-substack.headline: Play newsletter / Substack: paid attention
newsletter-substack.whoItsFor: Writers with a sharp niche and the patience to publish before the money shows up.
newsletter-substack.firstDollar: Often 6-24 months to meaningful paid subs. Free growth first, then a clear paid wedge.

steps.title: 1. Niche that pays
steps.body: B2B and career niches convert better than vague lifestyle. Readers should get career or money ROI.

steps.title: 2. Free issues that prove value
steps.body: Ship weekly. One free banger can outgrow months of quiet posts.

steps.title: 3. Paid tier with a sharp wall
steps.body: Archives, playbooks, community, or deep dives. Not 'support me' alone.

steps.title: 4. Cross-post for growth
steps.body: X, LinkedIn, SEO, podcast clips. Substack discovery helps but is not enough.

steps.title: 5. Add a second product later
steps.body: Courses, consulting, or community once the list trusts you.

digital-products-templates.headline: Play digital products: build once, sell many
digital-products-templates.whoItsFor: People who already know what a niche repeatedly needs: templates, kits, small tools, guides.
digital-products-templates.firstDollar: Days after listing if you have distribution. Near zero forever without traffic.

steps.title: 1. Steal demand, do not invent it
steps.body: Search Gumroad, Etsy, Twitter wishlists, client requests. Sell something people already buy.

steps.title: 2. Make the smallest product
steps.body: One template pack, not a 40-hour course. Ship this week.

steps.title: 3. Sales page with proof
steps.body: Screenshots, who it is for, what they get, refund policy. Price tests beat perfect branding.

steps.title: 4. Distribution loop
steps.body: SEO, short-form demos, newsletter, niche communities. Product without a pipe dies.

steps.title: 5. Iterate from refunds and questions
steps.body: Support tickets are a product roadmap.

pitfalls: Course-length scope for a template buyer

lead-generation-sites.headline: Play lead gen: traffic in, sold leads out
lead-generation-sites.whoItsFor: Operators who like conversion math more than personal branding. High margin when RPL beats CPL.
lead-generation-sites.firstDollar: SEO path: months. Paid path: days if tests work, expensive if they do not.

steps.title: 1. Pick a vertical with hungry buyers
steps.body: Insurance, legal, home services, education, solar-like categories with real CPL markets.

steps.title: 2. Secure demand first
steps.body: Talk to buyers or networks who purchase leads. Know price and quality rules before you spend on traffic.

steps.title: 3. Build a converting front end
steps.body: Content site or landing + form. Compliance and disclosure matter. Track every source.

steps.title: 4. Buy or earn traffic carefully
steps.body: SEO compounds; paid is a science experiment. Kill losers in days, not months.

steps.title: 5. Protect margin
steps.body: Quality scores, refund rates, and seasonality will murder you if ignored. Spreadsheet the unit economics weekly.

pitfalls: Scaling a channel that is only barely profitable

affiliate-seo-content-sites.headline: Play affiliate SEO: content that ranks, then earns
affiliate-seo-content-sites.whoItsFor: Writers/operators patient enough for 6-18 month SEO timelines and honest enough to track rankings.
affiliate-seo-content-sites.firstDollar: Often 6-18 months. Many sites never rank. AI spam made the easy meta harder.

steps.title: 1. Keyword + affiliate fit
steps.body: Commercial intent queries with real programs (Amazon, software affiliates, finance where legal).

steps.title: 2. Publish useful pages
steps.body: Comparisons, tutorials, tools. Depth and original data beat spun AI sludge.

steps.title: 3. Technical SEO basics
steps.body: Speed, internal links, indexation, clean structure. Fix crawl waste.

steps.title: 4. Build topical authority
steps.body: Clusters, not random posts. Earn links with assets people cite.

steps.title: 5. Diversify monetization
steps.body: Add email, lead gen, or a product so one algorithm update cannot zero you.

ai-automation-agency.headline: Play AI automation agency: projects first, product later
ai-automation-agency.whoItsFor: Technical generalists who can integrate tools and talk to non-technical buyers.
ai-automation-agency.firstDollar: Days to weeks with outbound. Projects fund learning; retainers fund stability.

steps.title: 1. Demo three real automations
steps.body: Lead capture, support triage, reporting. Record Loom demos with clear ROI stories.

steps.title: 2. Offer a fixed pilot
steps.body: 2-week paid pilot with a defined success metric. Avoid open-ended discovery forever.

steps.title: 3. Outbound to SMBs
steps.body: Vertical lists. Speak operations language, not model names.

steps.title: 4. Productize delivery
steps.body: SOPs, reusable agents, monitoring. Move toward service-as-software retainers.

steps.title: 5. Fire bad-fit clients
steps.body: No access, no data, no budget = no project.

pitfalls: Clients who want magic with no process change

ugc-clipping.headline: Play UGC / clipping: edit fast, get paid per asset
ugc-clipping.whoItsFor: Editors who can turn product footage into scroll-stopping clips. 2026 entry meta.
ugc-clipping.firstDollar: Days if you cold-outreach with a free sample clip.

steps.title: 1. Build a 5-clip portfolio
steps.body: Re-edit public product videos as speculative samples. Show hook rate taste.

steps.title: 2. Price a simple package
steps.body: e.g. 8 clips / mo. Fixed scope. Delivery in CapCut or Premiere.

steps.title: 3. Outbound to brands and creators
steps.body: Send the sample, not a resume. Target brands already running paid social.

steps.title: 4. Systemize briefs
steps.body: Intake form, brand kit, revision rules. Speed is your moat.

steps.title: 5. Ladder to retainers or productized content
steps.body: Do not stay stuck at 20 USD clips forever.

pitfalls: Racing to the bottom on price
Clients with no creative direction and infinite revisions

high-ticket-consulting.headline: Play high-ticket consulting: few clients, deep work
high-ticket-consulting.whoItsFor: Experts with proof. Not for beginners cosplaying mentorship.
high-ticket-consulting.firstDollar: Weeks if you have a reputation and outbound. Never if you only have vibes.

steps.title: 1. Codify the outcome
steps.body: What changes for the client in 30-90 days. Price against that outcome, not your hours.

steps.title: 2. Proof page
steps.body: Case studies, metrics, who you refuse. Application form, not a free free-for-all calendar.

steps.title: 3. Conversations, not funnels first
steps.body: Warm network and targeted outreach. High ticket closes on calls.

steps.title: 4. Delivery container
steps.body: Calls, async reviews, scorecards. Protect your calendar.

steps.title: 5. Productize the common advice
steps.body: Turn repeated lessons into a course or community later.

pitfalls: Unlimited access offers that destroy margins

community-membership.headline: Play community / membership: recurring access
community-membership.whoItsFor: Operators who can host, moderate, and show up. Audience without hospitality dies.
community-membership.firstDollar: After you already have trust. Cold communities rarely monetize.

steps.title: 1. Define the room
steps.body: Who belongs, who does not, what members get weekly. 'Vibes' is not an offer.

steps.title: 2. Soft-launch with founders
steps.body: 10-50 paid or founding members. High touch. Learn rituals that keep people.

steps.title: 3. Cadence over content dumps
steps.body: Office hours, wins threads, challenges. Empty Discord is a refund machine.

steps.title: 4. Instrument churn
steps.body: Why people leave. Fix onboarding before you buy ads.

steps.title: 5. Add tiers carefully
steps.body: Core membership first. High-ticket rooms only after core is healthy.

chrome-extension-tools.headline: Play Chrome extensions: tiny tool, store distribution
chrome-extension-tools.whoItsFor: Devs who can ship a focused utility and grind store SEO + reviews.
chrome-extension-tools.firstDollar: Weeks to months after listing. Most extensions never monetize.

steps.title: 1. One workflow pain
steps.body: Something people do daily in the browser. Save clicks, scrape carefully, enforce checklists.

steps.title: 2. Ship freemium v1
steps.body: Useful free tier, clear paid unlock. Privacy policy required.

steps.title: 3. Store listing craft
steps.body: Screenshots, tight copy, keywords. Reviews are oxygen.

steps.title: 4. Support without drowning
steps.body: FAQ, templates, scope limits. Extensions attract edge cases.

steps.title: 5. Cross-sell into SaaS if it fits
steps.body: The extension can be the top of funnel for a bigger product.

amazon-fba-e-commerce-store.headline: Play Amazon FBA: product, inventory, ops
amazon-fba-e-commerce-store.whoItsFor: Operators okay with inventory risk and Amazon's rules. Not passive.
amazon-fba-e-commerce-store.firstDollar: Often 3-12 months including research, samples, and first inbound shipment.

steps.title: 1. Validate demand and margin
steps.body: Tools + sold history. Need real margin after Amazon fees, ads, returns, and freight.

steps.title: 2. Source carefully
steps.body: Samples, QC, MOQ math. Do not order a container on a hunch.

steps.title: 3. Launch with a plan
steps.body: Listing, A+ content, initial reviews within policy, PPC budget.

steps.title: 4. Operate the P&L weekly
steps.body: TACoS, stockouts, and returns will decide if you keep the SKU.

steps.title: 5. Diversify risk
steps.body: Second channel or brand site when one SKU works. Amazon can change the rules.

online-courses-cohorts.headline: Play courses / cohorts: teach what already works for you
online-courses-cohorts.whoItsFor: People with a proven skill and an audience (or a way to borrow one).
online-courses-cohorts.firstDollar: Can be fast with a waitlist and a live cohort. Async catalogs are slower and more competitive.

steps.title: 1. Teach a result you have delivered
steps.body: Not a topic you find interesting. A transformation with proof.

steps.title: 2. Sell the cohort before filming everything
steps.body: Live first cohort, rough notes okay. Record and polish after revenue.

steps.title: 3. Curriculum as milestones
steps.body: Weekly outcomes, not 40 hours of video filler.

steps.title: 4. Distribution is the product
steps.body: Email, partnerships, YouTube. Marketplace alone is a race.

steps.title: 5. Turn FAQs into assets
steps.body: Templates, community, or consulting upsells for people who want more.

pitfalls: Teaching theory you have not practiced

marketing-agency.headline: Play marketing agency: sell results, hire delivery later
marketing-agency.whoItsFor: Operators who can sell and manage clients. Skill alone is not enough.
marketing-agency.firstDollar: Weeks with outbound if you can show proof. Delivery capacity is the constraint after sales works.

steps.title: 1. Niche + offer
steps.body: One vertical, one channel (SEO, paid, lifecycle, creative). Generalist agencies drown.

steps.title: 2. Case study engine
steps.body: Even one strong result with numbers beats a moodboard.

steps.title: 3. Sales motion
steps.body: Outbound + referrals. Packages with clear KPIs and kill-switches.

steps.title: 4. Delivery system
steps.body: SOPs, reporting cadence, contractor bench. You are an ops company that markets.

steps.title: 5. Margin discipline
steps.body: Fire bad clients. Raise prices. Avoid becoming a body shop with no leverage.

b2b-saas-sales.headline: Play B2B SaaS sales: get paid to learn closing
b2b-saas-sales.whoItsFor: High-agency people who want fast cash, skills, and a ladder into owning an offer later.
b2b-saas-sales.firstDollar: Weeks after hire once you ramp. Base + commission; OTE is not a guarantee.

steps.title: 1. Get the seat
steps.body: Target growing SaaS companies. Practice discovery calls and objection handling before interviews.

steps.title: 2. Learn the product and ICP
steps.body: Who buys, why now, what competitors they consider. Shadow top reps.

steps.title: 3. Pipeline hygiene
steps.body: Activity metrics matter early. Quality notes and next steps matter always.

steps.title: 4. Close and document
steps.body: Write your own playbook from wins. That IP is portable.

steps.title: 5. Optional exit ramps
steps.body: Move into founding sales for a startup, or use the skill to sell your own service/SaaS.

pitfalls: Joining a product that cannot sell

software-developers.headline: Play software developer: employment as a cash engine
software-developers.whoItsFor: People willing to learn fundamentals and ship. Strong start-now path with verifiable pay.
software-developers.firstDollar: Months to a few years depending on background. Bootcamp promises vary wildly.

steps.title: 1. Fundamentals over frameworks
steps.body: One language deep, data structures enough to interview, git, and SQL.

steps.title: 2. Portfolio of real ships
steps.body: Deployed projects beat tutorial clones. Prefer tools people use.

steps.title: 3. Interview loop reps
steps.body: LeetCode enough to not panic, system design basics, behavioral stories.

steps.title: 4. Land the job, keep learning
steps.body: First role is a wedge. Optimize for mentors and code ownership.

steps.title: 5. Optional leverage path
steps.body: Side projects, freelancing, or micro-SaaS once cash flow is stable.

big-tech-swe-equity.headline: Play big-tech SWE: cash + equity ceiling
big-tech-swe-equity.whoItsFor: Strong engineers who will grind interviews. Highest ceiling employment deck on many boards.
big-tech-swe-equity.firstDollar: After you clear interviews. Prep is months; the offer is the first real dollar jump.

steps.title: 1. Level honestly
steps.body: Target companies and levels that match your experience. Levels.fyi for ranges, not as destiny.

steps.title: 2. Interview machine
steps.body: Daily DS&A, weekly system design, mock interviews. Treat it like a sport season.

steps.title: 3. Narrative and resume
steps.body: Impact metrics, scope, leadership signals. Referrals beat cold apply.

steps.title: 4. Negotiate total comp
steps.body: Base, bonus, equity, sign-on. Competing offers matter.

steps.title: 5. Do not outsource your life to RSUs
steps.body: Equity is volatile. Keep skills sharp and options open.

electrician.headline: Play electrician: paid apprenticeship into high skill trade
electrician.whoItsFor: People who want hands-on work, clear training path, and strong median pay without a 4-year degree.
electrician.firstDollar: Apprenticeship wages start during training. Journey-level pay after 4-5 years.

steps.title: 1. Find a real apprenticeship
steps.body: IBEW/NECA or non-union contractors. Apply early; slots are competitive.

steps.title: 2. Get in and stay safe
steps.body: Show up, learn code, treat journeymen as mentors. Safety is non-negotiable.

steps.title: 3. Collect hours and license path
steps.body: Track OJT hours toward journeyman exam. Know your state board rules.

steps.title: 4. Specialize for ceiling
steps.body: Industrial, controls, solar, data centers. Specialization raises rates.

steps.title: 5. Optional shop path
steps.body: Master license + van + insurance if you want owner-operator.

plumber-pipefitter.headline: Play plumber: apprenticeship, license, then route or shop
plumber-pipefitter.whoItsFor: Hands-on operators who want recession-resilient demand and a path to ownership.
plumber-pipefitter.firstDollar: Apprentice wages during training; service calls pay once licensed.

steps.title: 1. Enter apprenticeship or helper role
steps.body: Union or reputable shop. Helpers can convert if hours count.

steps.title: 2. Master service fundamentals
steps.body: Drain, water heater, rough-in. Speed and cleanliness win callbacks.

steps.title: 3. License on schedule
steps.body: State journeyman then master if ownership is the goal.

steps.title: 4. Build a book of business
steps.body: Google reviews, maintenance plans, relationships with GCs.

steps.title: 5. One-truck shop if ready
steps.body: Insurance, bonding, truck stock. Price for callbacks.

hvac-technician.headline: Play HVAC tech: certs, then service or install path
hvac-technician.whoItsFor: Mechanical tinkerers who want high demand and a route to a service company.
hvac-technician.firstDollar: Often within months via school + helper; EPA 608 required for refrigerants.

steps.title: 1. Get EPA 608 and basic training
steps.body: Trade school or manufacturer path. Safety first.

steps.title: 2. Join a service company
steps.body: Install vs service: service often teaches diagnostics faster.

steps.title: 3. Stack manufacturer certs
steps.body: Brand certs raise your value to shops and customers.

steps.title: 4. Track your close rate on options
steps.body: Maintenance agreements and equipment changeouts.

steps.title: 5. Owner path optional
steps.body: One truck, then techs. Capital and dispatch systems matter.

ghostwriting-copywriting.headline: Play ghostwriting: niche words for other peoples brands
ghostwriting-copywriting.whoItsFor: Strong writers who prefer cash and craft over personal fame.
ghostwriting-copywriting.firstDollar: Weeks with outbound and 2-3 samples. Niche (SaaS, health, finance) pays more.

steps.title: 1. Pick a niche and format
steps.body: LinkedIn ghost, newsletters, sales pages, or books. One lane first.

steps.title: 2. Portfolio of speculative pieces
steps.body: Write in the voice of target clients. Show before/after metrics if possible.

steps.title: 3. Outbound to buyers
steps.body: Founders, agencies, thought leaders. Send a free sample opener.

steps.title: 4. Productize packages
steps.body: Monthly ghost retainer beats one-off blog posts.

steps.title: 5. Raise rates with proof
steps.body: Publish anonymized case studies. Fire low-pay marketplaces when full.

virtual-assistant.headline: Play VA: reliability is the product
virtual-assistant.whoItsFor: Organized operators who want remote retainers and a ladder into specialized ops.
virtual-assistant.firstDollar: Days to weeks via marketplaces or cold outreach with a clear menu.

steps.title: 1. Offer a tight menu
steps.body: Inbox, calendar, research, customer support. Not everything.

steps.title: 2. Tools fluency
steps.body: Gmail, Notion, Slack, basic Zapier. Show SOPs you follow.

steps.title: 3. Land one retainer
steps.body: 20 hours/mo beats scattershot gigs.

steps.title: 4. Specialize
steps.body: Podcast VA, ecom VA, real estate VA. Higher rates.

steps.title: 5. Productize or agency
steps.body: Templates and junior VAs if you want leverage.

web-dev-freelance.headline: Play freelance web dev: portfolio over credentials
web-dev-freelance.whoItsFor: Builders who can ship and sell. Closest cash path for coders outside W2.
web-dev-freelance.firstDollar: Weeks with a clear offer (landing pages, Next.js apps, Webflow).

steps.title: 1. One stack, one offer
steps.body: e.g. marketing sites in Next.js or Webflow for local services.

steps.title: 2. Three live portfolio pieces
steps.body: Deployed, with outcomes if possible.

steps.title: 3. Outbound weekly
steps.body: Agencies and local businesses. Fixed packages beat open bids early.

steps.title: 4. Protect scope
steps.body: Written SOW, change orders, deposits.

steps.title: 5. Ladder
steps.body: Productized builds, retainers, or your own SaaS.

pitfalls: building products for free under client IP

influencer-brand-deals.headline: Play influencer deals: audience first, media kit second
influencer-brand-deals.whoItsFor: Creators who already have engaged niche attention. Not a cold-start career.
influencer-brand-deals.firstDollar: After consistent audience; first deals are often free product or low pay.

steps.title: 1. Niche audience with proof
steps.body: Engagement rate over vanity followers.

steps.title: 2. Media kit
steps.body: Demographics, past work, rates, deliverables.

steps.title: 3. Outbound + platforms
steps.body: Brands in your niche; do not wait for inbounds only.

steps.title: 4. Own a backend offer
steps.body: Email list or product so platform cuts do not own you.

steps.title: 5. Diversify
steps.body: Multiple brands, multiple platforms, owned product.

amazon-kdp-self-publishing.headline: Play KDP: catalog and ads, not one viral book
amazon-kdp-self-publishing.whoItsFor: Writers/operators who can treat publishing like a product business.
amazon-kdp-self-publishing.firstDollar: Months; ads can accelerate or burn cash.

steps.title: 1. Validate categories
steps.body: Look at BSR and competition before writing 80k words.

steps.title: 2. Ship a tight first title
steps.body: Editing and cover matter more than perfect prose early.

steps.title: 3. Launch with a plan
steps.body: Keywords, categories, optional ads with hard ROAS stops.

steps.title: 4. Build a catalog
steps.body: Series and related titles compound.

steps.title: 5. Own an email list
steps.body: Do not rely only on Amazon discoverability.

chick-fil-a-operator.headline: Play Chick-fil-A operator: selection is the boss fight
chick-fil-a-operator.whoItsFor: Operators who want a proven system, can pass a brutal selection process, and will run the store full-time.
chick-fil-a-operator.firstDollar: After selection and opening; multi-year process is common.

steps.title: 1. Study the model
steps.body: Single-unit, operator-in-restaurant. Not passive ownership.

steps.title: 2. Strengthen the application
steps.body: Leadership proof, hospitality track record, financial readiness.

steps.title: 3. Survive selection
steps.body: Multiple interviews; acceptance rate is extremely low.

steps.title: 4. Train in the system
steps.body: Corporate path after selection.

steps.title: 5. Operate excellence daily
steps.body: People, speed, cleanliness. The brand is the product.

pitfalls: treating it like a passive franchise
